Researchers King's College London translating research eating disorders during pregnancy motherhood into practical training resources, helping healthcare professionals provide best care pregnant women mothers.
Launching today start Eating Disorders Awareness Week 2018, resources include animation co-designed research team, women with lived experience, healthcare professionals organisations.
Currently there very limited resources available healthcare professionals, despite previous research finding that approximately 7.5% women suffering from eating disorder during pregnancy.
animation aims raise awareness that eating disorders serious mental illnesses that impact pregnancy beyond, highlights importance trusting relationship between women suffering from eating disorders healthcare professionals.
'Women with eating disorders often reluctant disclose their illness healthcare professionals, possibly fear stigma, healthcare professionals unsure about identify women with eating disorders what support they need,' says Abigail Easter, Senior Postdoctoral Fellow Improvement Science King's College London's Institute Psychiatry, Psychology Neuroscience (IoPPN).
Recent guidance from National Institute Health Care Excellence (NICE, 2017) recommendations enhanced monitoring support women with eating disorders before, during after pregnancy.
However, barriers exist which prevent healthcare professionals from implementing current NICE guidelines practice.
animation will will hosted online alongside additional training resources, will presented training event IoPPN Tuesday 27th March.
project funded through Health Foundation's Evidence into Practice programme, Easter supported King's Improvement Science, part Centre Implementation Science within NIHR Collaboration Leadership Applied Health Research Care (CLAHRC) South London.

Photography Centre opens, more than doubling space dedicated photography spanning four galleries.
centre opened 12th October with major commissions month-long Photography Spotlight.
world's first photographic experiments earliest cameras.
Works pioneering female photographers including Julia Margaret Cameron, pictures 20th-century greats Cecil Beaton Irving Penn.
Contemporary works Martin Parr, Mary McCartney, Cornelia Parker Hiroshi Sugimoto.
Phase Photography Centre more than doubles space dedicated photography V&A, spanning four galleries.
opens with major display Collecting Photography: From Daguerreotype Digital, beginning newly named Bern Ronny Schwartz Gallery (formerly Gallery 100).
show explores photography 'collecting world', from medium's invention 19th century present day.
Drawn from V&A's collection over 800,000 photographs, display showcases some most exciting contemporary photography being created today.
collection established V&A's visionary first director Henry Cole spans entire history photography, telling story medium from daguerreotype digital.
shows seminal prints pioneers William Henry Talbot, Julia Margaret Cameron Roger Fenton, alongside negatives, camera equipment, photographic publications original documents tell broader story about history international photography.
Modern Media Gallery (formerly Gallery 99), frequently changing selection acquisitions, 'Light Wall' displaying screen-based photography, 'Dark Tent' projection area complete space.
mark opening, commissioned artists produce major works.
German photographer Thomas Ruff, known taking critical conceptual approach photography, created monumental series inspired Linnaeus Tripe's 1850s paper negatives India Burma, held V&A's collection.
Alongside Thomas Ruff's series, American artist Penelope Umbrico created Clouds from Online Collection, 1630 1885, 2018, first work feature Light Wall.
Umbrico works mostly with images finds internet, presenting them ways that reveal fluidity digital photography.
work explores transition from fleeting clouds material paint, then from digital code physical screen.
Visitors enter Photography Centre through spectacular installation over cameras spanning years.
Nearby, interactive camera handling station offers visitors understanding photographers view world through their equipment.
Inside gallery, focused sections look series collections collectors.
Photography Centre also features Dark Tent, flexible multimedia projection lecture space inspired 19th-century photographers' travelling darkrooms.
Here, specially commissioned films revealing early photographic processes, including daguerreotype, calotype collodion process, screened, along with slideshow rarely-seen magic lantern slides revealing first attempts reach summit Mount Everest 1921 1922, among other photographic projections.
Situated V&A's North East Quarter, Photography Centre part V&A's FuturePlan development programme revitalise museum's public spaces through contemporary design restoration original features.
second phase Photography Centre, planned open 2022, will expand further, with ambitions include teaching research space, browsing library studio darkroom photographers' residencies.
